1: shadow For God alone my soul waits in silence; from him comes my salvation. Christ before Pilate embodies this silence: he gave him no answer, not even to a single charge, so that the governor was greatly amazed (Matthew 27:14; cf. Isaiah 53:7). His salvation comes not from human defense but from God alone — the resurrection is God's single act of vindication for the one who waits in silent trust.
2: shadow He alone is my rock and my salvation, my fortress; I shall not be greatly shaken. Christ is the rock — the rock was Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4). He is the one who shall not be shaken because God raised him: the resurrection is the ultimate steadying of the one whom death tried to shake. He who is the rock is also the one who rests on the rock in perfect trust.
3: shadow How long will all of you attack a man to batter him, like a leaning wall, a tottering fence? The sustained assault on the righteous man — from the Sanhedrin's arrest through Pilate's interrogation and the mockery at the cross — batters the one who appears like a tottering fence. Yet he is the cornerstone (Ephesians 2:20) who cannot ultimately fall, however violently he is struck.
4: shadow They only plan to thrust him down from his high position. They take pleasure in falsehood. They bless with their mouths, but inwardly they curse. The hypocrisy of those who hailed Jesus at the Triumphal Entry and then cried crucify him! five days later — blessing with mouths, cursing inwardly. Judas's kiss is the supreme instance: the gesture of friendship concealing betrayal.
5: shadow For God alone, O my soul, wait in silence, for my hope is from him. Christ is himself the hope: Christ in you, the hope of glory (Colossians 1:27). The soul that waits in silence for God waits for the one who is God's ultimate answer. Christ modeled this waiting — in Gethsemane, not my will, but yours, be done (Luke 22:42) — and became the ground of all waiting hope.
6: shadow He only is my rock and my salvation, my fortress; I shall not be shaken. The repeated refrain deepens with Christ's resurrection: the tomb could not hold him, the stone was rolled away (Matthew 28:2), and the one who rested in the rock-hewn tomb was raised. He is unmoved by death itself — the fortress that no enemy can storm.
7: shadow On God rests my salvation and my glory; my mighty rock, my refuge is God. Christ's glory rests on the Father's vindication through resurrection. He is simultaneously the one who takes refuge in God (as the righteous man) and the refuge itself (as the divine Son). Paul's Christ, who is our life (Colossians 3:4) and John's I am the resurrection and the life (John 11:25) both fulfill this verse.
8: theme Trust in him at all times, O people; pour out your heart before him; God is a refuge for us. The invitation to pour out the heart before God is the posture of prayer Christ both exemplified and enables. He poured out his soul to death (Isaiah 53:12); through him we have access to the Father by one Spirit (Ephesians 2:18), so that we may pour out our hearts at all times.
9: theme Those of low estate are but a breath; those of high estate are a delusion; in the balances they go up; they are together lighter than a breath. The vanity of human power — the Romans, the Sanhedrin, Pilate — all lighter than a breath against the purposes of God. Christ's kingdom cannot be shaken (Hebrews 12:28) while the empires that crucified him are dust. Human pretension is the backdrop against which divine power appears.
10: theme Put no trust in extortion; set no vain hopes on robbery; if riches increase, set not your heart on them. Christ's teaching on wealth and the heart (Matthew 6:19-21; Luke 12:15) echoes this wisdom. He himself had nowhere to lay his head (Matthew 8:20), renouncing every form of material security to reveal the kingdom that no theft can diminish.
11: shadow Once God has spoken; twice have I heard this: that power belongs to God, Divine power displayed supremely at the resurrection: the power of God (1 Corinthians 1:24) is Christ crucified and raised. The Father's power does not belong to Caesar or the Sanhedrin — it belongs to the one who raised Jesus from the dead and who will raise those who are in him (Romans 8:11).
12: direct and that to you, O Lord, belongs steadfast love. For you will render to a man according to his work. Paul cites this verse in Romans 2:6 (rendering to each according to works) and Christ applies it to himself: Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me, to repay each one for what he has done (Revelation 22:12). The one who renders according to works is the risen Christ who holds the final judgment.
